turns.content: I'm struggling to distinguish between opt-level and debug settings in my Cargo profiles. I know that the default release profile uses opt-level=3, but how does this compare to the standard library's behavior when building with cargo build --release versus just running the binary?

The key difference lies in how the compiler handles function calls and constants. When you use opt-level=3, the Rustc compiler aggressively inlines functions that are safe to inline and removes dead code after optimization passes run. In contrast, a standard debug build prioritizes readability over speed, often leaving out optimizations like constant propagation or tail-call elimination. For your interview prep, remember that the release profile specifically disables some safety checks like stack unwinding, which means if a panic occurs at runtime with panic = "abort", it will terminate immediately instead of propagating up to the handler.

I see, but I'm confused about how codegen-units interacts with opt-level here. If I set opt-level=3 and also enable -C link-arg=-Wl,-O2, does that actually change the final binary size compared to just using opt-level? And what happens if I run cargo build --release without explicitly setting these flags?

You're right to question this because the default behavior of cargo build --release is exactly what you want for most applications. It automatically sets opt-level=3 and enables LTO (link-time optimization) by default, which significantly reduces binary size by merging functions across crates before linking. However, if you manually add -C link-arg=-Wl,-O2 without also ensuring the other profile settings are correct, you might end up with a binary that looks smaller but performs worse because you've disabled certain optimizations needed for LTO to work effectively. For your interview, focus on how Cargo profiles bundle these flags together so you can control trade-offs between performance and size in one go.

I'm trying to optimize my binary with cargo but I keep getting the wrong size. If I set opt-level = 3 and panic = "abort" in my profile, why does it sometimes strip symbols that I actually need for debugging later?

That happens because opt-level=3 combined with strip=symbols removes the symbol table entirely. You can keep debug info by setting strip=codes only, which leaves the DWARF data you need while still enabling aggressive LTO and codegen-units optimizations.
But I thought opt-level=3 was just about instruction selection. What exactly does that do compared to opt-level=2, and why would the Rust compiler actually reorder functions when I enable LTO?

Opt-level 3 allows the optimizer to perform aggressive dead code elimination and function inlining across crate boundaries during compilation, which is what lets it merge libraries into your final binary. When you enable LTO, the Rust compiler reorders functions globally before linking so that call sites can be optimized away entirely if their targets are eliminated.
I'm getting confused about the difference between opt-level and debug when setting up profiles. It seems like I just need to pick one, but why does it matter so much for performance?

It's totally normal to feel lost here; the distinction is what separates a usable library from an optimized binary. You're looking at two different optimization strategies: opt-level controls how aggressively the compiler simplifies code, while debug keeps symbols and source mapping intact. For your release profile, you'd set opt-level to 3 for maximum speed, but if you need profiling or debugging later, you keep it at 1 or 2 instead of letting the optimizer remove everything.
Okay so I can just toggle that flag? But what about lto and codegen-units? Do those change how things compile vs run?

Those are compile-time settings that affect how your code is assembled, not runtime behavior. LTO merges functions across crates before linking to reduce instruction count, while codegen-units limits how many separate chunks the compiler generates; increasing this value helps parallel compilation but might slightly impact performance if set too high. You usually keep lto enabled and codegen-units at 1 or 2 for most projects unless you're building a massive library.
What about strip and panic = abort? Are those just extra flags I need to add manually?

Those are part of the profile itself, not separate commands. strip removes debug symbols and unused code from the final binary, making it smaller and faster, while panic = abort sets how your program handles panics by terminating immediately instead of unwinding the stack. You don't add them manually; you define them in [profile.release] so cargo knows to apply all these options together during the build.
Got it. So if I want a tiny binary for production, what's the best combination?

For a tiny binary, use opt-level 3 with lto enabled and codegen-units set low, then add strip = true to remove unnecessary metadata. Also set panic = abort so your app exits cleanly on errors without growing the stack. This combo gives you maximum compression and speed for production builds.
